title: RF Math, Decibels & Link Budgets
summary: How embassy radio engineers use decibels, link budgets, and basic electrical power math to predict whether a circuit will close and to size site power.
content:
The decibel (dB) is a logarithmic ratio comparing two power levels: dB = 10 × log10(P_out / P_in). It is a ratio, not an absolute value. Radio uses dB because RF signals span an enormous range (a transmitter may be billions of times stronger than the signal a receiver hears), and because gains and losses cascaded along a path simply ADD and SUBTRACT in dB instead of multiplying — far easier mental math for a watch officer planning a link.

Memorize the key dB shortcuts: +3 dB = double the power (×2), −3 dB = half the power. +10 dB = ten times the power (×10), −10 dB = one tenth. Combine them: +6 dB = ×4 (3+3), +20 dB = ×100, +23 dB ≈ ×200. These let you do power math in your head without a calculator.

To make dB an ABSOLUTE measurement you add a reference. dBm is power referenced to 1 milliwatt (0 dBm = 1 mW). dBW is referenced to 1 watt (0 dBW = 1 W, and 30 dBm = 0 dBW = 1 W). Quick conversions: 0 dBm = 1 mW, +10 dBm = 10 mW, +20 dBm = 100 mW, +30 dBm = 1 W, +40 dBm = 10 W, +43 dBm = 20 W. Going down: −30 dBm = 1 microwatt, and a typical receiver might hear signals near −100 dBm or weaker.

A LINK BUDGET is the accounting sheet for a radio circuit. The basic equation: Received power (dBm) = TX output power + TX antenna gain − feedline/connector losses (TX side) − path loss + RX antenna gain − feedline/connector losses (RX side). If the received power exceeds the receiver's SENSITIVITY (the minimum signal it needs, e.g. −110 dBm), the link closes. Everything is in dB/dBm so you literally add and subtract down the page.

FADE MARGIN is the safety buffer: received power minus receiver sensitivity. A circuit that arrives exactly at sensitivity will drop out the moment conditions worsen (rain, fading, foliage, a passing vehicle). Diplomatic links are typically designed with 10–20 dB or more of fade margin so they stay up under degraded conditions and during a crisis.

FREE-SPACE PATH LOSS (FSPL) is the dominant loss term and grows with both distance and frequency. The intuition: every time you DOUBLE the distance you lose ~6 dB, and every time you DOUBLE the frequency you also lose ~6 dB. This is why higher bands (SHF/satellite) need bigger antennas and more power for the same range, and why a longer link needs proportionally more margin. Real paths add further losses from terrain, buildings, rain, and foliage on top of FSPL.

The electrical side rests on Ohm's law and the power equations. Ohm's law: V = I × R (volts = amps × ohms). Power: P = V × I, and by substitution P = I² × R and P = V² / R. A radio drawing more current on transmit than receive is why TX power budgeting matters for batteries and generators.

For site power sizing, work in amp-hours and watt-hours. Estimate average current draw across the RX/TX duty cycle, multiply by hours of required autonomy to get amp-hours of battery, then add derating (batteries are not run to 0%, and lead-acid is typically used to ~50% depth of discharge). For solar, size the panel to replace the daily watt-hours consumed PLUS losses, using realistic local 'sun hours,' so an unattended HF or VHF relay site survives cloudy days and the longest night.
keyPoints: dB is a logarithmic RATIO: dB = 10·log10(P2/P1); gains/losses cascade by addition, which is why RF math is done in dB
+3 dB doubles power, −3 dB halves it; +10 dB = ×10, +20 dB = ×100, +6 dB = ×4
dBm references 1 mW (0 dBm = 1 mW); dBW references 1 W; 30 dBm = 0 dBW = 1 W
dBm-to-watts ladder: 0 dBm=1 mW, 20 dBm=100 mW, 30 dBm=1 W, 40 dBm=10 W
Link budget: RX power = TX power + gains − losses − path loss; link closes if RX power > receiver sensitivity
Fade margin = received power − receiver sensitivity; diplomatic links are built with generous margin (often 10–20+ dB)
Free-space path loss intuition: ~6 dB more loss each time distance doubles, and ~6 dB each time frequency doubles
Ohm's law V=I·R; power P=V·I = I²·R = V²/R
Site power sizing uses amp-hours/watt-hours with derating; lead-acid typically used to ~50% depth of discharge
Solar sizing must replace daily watt-hours plus losses using realistic local sun hours for unattended sites

flashcards.front: What is a decibel (dB)?
flashcards.back: A logarithmic ratio of two power levels: dB = 10·log10(P_out/P_in). It is a ratio, not an absolute value.

flashcards.front: Why does radio use dB instead of raw power ratios?
flashcards.back: RF spans a huge dynamic range, and in dB cascaded gains and losses simply add and subtract instead of multiplying.

flashcards.front: How much power change is +3 dB? −3 dB?
flashcards.back: +3 dB = double the power (×2); −3 dB = half the power.

flashcards.front: How much power change is +10 dB and +20 dB?
What does +6 dB represent?
What does dBm reference, and what is 0 dBm?
flashcards.back: dBm references 1 milliwatt; 0 dBm = 1 mW.

flashcards.front: Convert 30 dBm to watts (and to dBW).
flashcards.back: 30 dBm = 1 watt = 0 dBW.

flashcards.front: What is 40 dBm in watts?
Write the basic link-budget equation.
flashcards.back: RX power = TX power + antenna gains − feedline/connector losses − path loss. Link closes if RX power > receiver sensitivity.

flashcards.front: What is receiver sensitivity?
flashcards.back: The minimum signal power a receiver needs to demodulate usable signal (e.g., around −100 to −110 dBm).

flashcards.front: What is fade margin and why does it matter?
flashcards.back: Received power minus receiver sensitivity — the safety buffer that keeps a link up during fading, rain, or degraded conditions.

flashcards.front: Free-space path loss intuition: doubling distance costs how much?
flashcards.back: About 6 dB of additional loss; doubling frequency also adds about 6 dB.

flashcards.front: State Ohm's law and the power equations.
flashcards.back: V = I·R; P = V·I = I²·R = V²/R.

flashcards.front: How do you size a battery for a radio site?
flashcards.back: Estimate average current draw, multiply by required autonomy hours for amp-hours, then derate (lead-acid typically used to ~50% depth of discharge).

flashcards.front: What must a solar panel replace at an unattended radio site?
flashcards.back: The full daily watt-hours consumed plus system losses, using realistic local sun hours, with reserve for cloudy days.

title: Interference, EMC & Spectrum Management
summary: Identifying and mitigating RF interference and emissions problems at a post, and managing the spectrum the mission is authorized to use.
content:
Radio Frequency Interference (RFI) and Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) are unwanted signals that degrade reception. They come from external transmitters, from other equipment at the same site, and even from a system interfering with itself. At a busy embassy compound with many radios, generators, IT gear, and nearby commercial transmitters, recognizing the TYPE of interference is the first step to fixing it.

Key interference mechanisms: INTERMODULATION ('intermod') occurs when two or more strong signals mix in a non-linear stage (often an overdriven amplifier or a corroded 'rusty bolt' junction) and produce new sum-and-difference frequencies that can land on a victim channel. HARMONICS are integer multiples of a transmitter's fundamental frequency (2×, 3×…) that spill energy into higher bands. DESENSITIZATION ('desense') is when a strong nearby off-channel signal overloads a receiver's front end, raising its effective noise floor so it goes 'deaf' to weak wanted signals.

Channel-relationship problems: CO-CHANNEL interference is another user on the exact same frequency (common with skywave-propagated distant stations or reused repeater frequencies). ADJACENT-CHANNEL interference is energy spilling from a transmitter on the next channel over, worsened by wide deviation, poor filtering, or channels packed too close together.

Mitigation techniques fall into shielding, filtering, and bonding. SHIELDING uses conductive enclosures and braided cable shields to keep fields in or out. FILTERS pass the wanted band and reject the rest — bandpass filters at a receiver, low-pass/harmonic filters at a transmitter, notch/band-reject filters to kill a specific offender, and duplexers/cavity filters at co-located repeater sites. FERRITE chokes/beads clamped on cables suppress common-mode currents that turn cables into unintended antennas — a cheap fix for 'RF in the shack' and computer-to-radio noise.

GROUNDING and BONDING are central to EMC. A single-point, low-impedance ground and bonding all equipment chassis and shields to a common ground bus prevents ground loops, controls static and lightning energy, and gives interference currents a defined return path. Poor bonding is a leading cause of mysterious noise and is also a safety issue.

EMANATION SECURITY (EMSEC), governed in the U.S. context by TEMPEST standards, addresses a different threat: equipment unintentionally radiates compromising emanations (signals that leak the plaintext or keystrokes being processed) which a hostile party could capture from outside the controlled space. Awareness means using TEMPEST-certified or shielded equipment for classified processing, enforcing RED/BLACK separation (keeping cabling/equipment that carries plaintext physically and electrically separated from that carrying ciphertext or going outside), and maintaining inspectable space — a core concern at every diplomatic post.

The ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM is a finite shared resource, divided into ALLOCATIONS (which services — broadcast, aeronautical, maritime, fixed, mobile, satellite, etc. — may use which frequency ranges). The ITU divides the world into three REGIONS (Region 1: Europe/Africa/Middle East/former USSR; Region 2: the Americas; Region 3: Asia-Pacific) with somewhat different allocation tables, so a frequency plan that is legal in one region may not be in another. Spectrum is also split into LICENSED bands (you must hold an assignment/license, with interference protection) versus UNLICENSED bands (e.g., common ISM bands used by Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, no protection — you must accept interference).

At a post this becomes frequency ASSIGNMENT and DECONFLICTION: obtaining host-nation authorization for each frequency, maintaining a frequency record/plan, and arranging channels so the mission's own radios, security nets, and devices do not interfere with each other or with host services. Finally, distinguish unintentional INTERFERENCE from deliberate JAMMING (intentional transmission to deny a channel). Both raise the noise floor, but jamming is hostile. Mitigation overlaps: change frequency, use directional antennas and nulling to reduce the offender, lower transmitter–receiver geometry exposure, employ frequency-hopping/spread-spectrum or encrypted resilient waveforms, and report/coordinate the event up the chain.
keyPoints: RFI/EMI is unwanted energy degrading reception; identify the mechanism before mitigating
Intermod = two strong signals mixing in a non-linear stage to create new frequencies; harmonics = integer multiples of a fundamental
Desensitization = a strong off-channel signal overloading the receiver front end so it can't hear weak signals
Co-channel = same frequency interference; adjacent-channel = spillover from the neighboring channel
Mitigation toolkit: shielding (enclosures/braid), filtering (bandpass/low-pass/notch, duplexers/cavities), and ferrite chokes on cables
Good EMC requires single-point, low-impedance grounding and bonding all chassis/shields to a common bus to avoid ground loops
TEMPEST/EMSEC addresses compromising emanations; use shielded gear and enforce RED/BLACK separation for classified processing
Spectrum is allocated by service; the ITU splits the world into Regions 1 (EMEA), 2 (Americas), 3 (Asia-Pacific) with differing tables
Licensed bands carry interference protection; unlicensed/ISM bands (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) carry none — you accept interference
Jamming is deliberate denial vs. accidental interference; mitigate by changing frequency, using directional/nulling antennas, spread-spectrum, and reporting up the chain

flashcards.front: Difference between RFI and EMI?
flashcards.back: Both are unwanted electromagnetic energy degrading a system; RFI specifically refers to radio-frequency interference, a subset of EMI.

flashcards.front: What is intermodulation (intermod)?
flashcards.back: Two or more strong signals mixing in a non-linear stage, producing new sum-and-difference frequencies that can fall on a victim channel.

flashcards.front: What are harmonics?
flashcards.back: Unwanted signals at integer multiples (2×, 3×, …) of a transmitter's fundamental frequency.

flashcards.front: What is receiver desensitization?
flashcards.back: A strong nearby off-channel signal overloads the receiver front end, raising its noise floor so it can't hear weak wanted signals.

flashcards.front: Co-channel vs. adjacent-channel interference?
flashcards.back: Co-channel = interference on the exact same frequency; adjacent-channel = energy spilling in from the next channel over.

flashcards.front: What does a ferrite choke/bead do?
flashcards.back: Suppresses common-mode currents on cables that would otherwise act as unintended antennas — a cheap fix for cable-coupled noise.

flashcards.front: Name three filter types used for interference control.
flashcards.back: Bandpass (at receivers), low-pass/harmonic (at transmitters), and notch/band-reject (kill a specific offender); duplexers/cavities at co-located sites.

flashcards.front: Why are grounding and bonding important for EMC?
flashcards.back: A single-point, low-impedance ground bonding all chassis and shields prevents ground loops, controls static/lightning, and gives interference a defined return path.

flashcards.front: What is TEMPEST / EMSEC concerned with?
flashcards.back: Compromising emanations — equipment unintentionally radiating signals that could leak classified data to an outside eavesdropper.

flashcards.front: What is RED/BLACK separation?
flashcards.back: Keeping equipment and cabling carrying plaintext (RED) physically and electrically separated from those carrying ciphertext or leaving the secure area (BLACK).

flashcards.front: What is a spectrum allocation?
flashcards.back: The designation of which radio services may use which frequency ranges, set in international and national tables.

flashcards.front: Name the three ITU Regions.
flashcards.back: Region 1: Europe/Africa/Middle East/former USSR; Region 2: the Americas; Region 3: Asia-Pacific.

flashcards.front: Licensed vs. unlicensed spectrum?
flashcards.back: Licensed bands require an assignment and carry interference protection; unlicensed/ISM bands (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) have no protection — you must accept interference.

flashcards.front: What is frequency deconfliction at a post?
flashcards.back: Arranging authorized frequencies so the mission's own nets and devices don't interfere with each other or with host-nation services.

flashcards.front: How does jamming differ from interference, and how is it mitigated?
flashcards.back: Jamming is deliberate denial of a channel; mitigate by changing frequency, using directional/nulling antennas, spread-spectrum/encrypted waveforms, and reporting up the chain.

title: HF Operations, NVIS & Data Modes in Practice
summary: Running practical HF voice and data circuits at a post — choosing frequencies, using ALE and data modes, picking antennas, and maintaining net discipline.
content:
HF is the backbone of long-haul, infrastructure-independent diplomatic communications: it works without satellites, repeaters, or host telecom, making it the reliable fallback in the PACE plan when primary networks fail. But HF is only as good as frequency selection, because the ionosphere that carries it changes constantly.

AUTOMATIC LINK ESTABLISHMENT (ALE) automates the hardest part of HF. An ALE-equipped radio continuously sounds and scores a pool of authorized frequencies, then automatically selects the best channel to reach a called station — handling the frequency-management problem that once required a skilled operator. Stations are addressed by ALE 'addresses,' and the controller picks the channel with the best measured link quality (often reported as a Link Quality Analysis score), greatly improving the odds of getting through.

Manual frequency selection follows the propagation rules: use HIGHER frequencies by day and LOWER frequencies by night, because daytime ionization raises the usable frequencies and they collapse after sunset. Season and the solar cycle matter too — higher SOLAR FLUX (more sunspots) supports higher usable frequencies and better long-haul propagation; low flux and winter nights push you down in frequency. Operators keep a day/night frequency pair (and seasonal variants) for each circuit and work near, but safely below, the MUF.

PROPAGATION PREDICTION tools turn this into planning. Programs and online services (using ionospheric models driven by solar indices like the smoothed sunspot number and the solar flux index) predict the optimum working frequency between two locations by hour. Operators also use real-time ionospheric soundings and beacon reports to confirm which band is open right now before committing traffic.

For HF DATA and EMAIL, modern systems use error-correcting protocols. ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) modes detect errors and request retransmission of bad blocks, delivering reliable text and email over a noisy, fading channel — the basis of HF email/messaging systems used for position reports, traffic, and store-and-forward when no internet exists. These data waveforms trade speed for robustness and pair naturally with ALE for unattended, automatic linking.

ANTENNA CHOICE depends on the mission. For NVIS (Near Vertical Incidence Skywave) — reliable regional coverage out to a few hundred kilometers with no skip zone — use a low horizontal antenna (roughly 0.1–0.25 wavelength high, e.g., a low dipole or purpose-built NVIS antenna) on the lower HF bands so energy goes nearly straight up and rains back down over the surrounding region; ideal for in-country and crisis nets across rough terrain. For long-haul DX, use higher, directional antennas (beams) or those favoring low takeoff angles to launch energy toward the horizon for multi-hop intercontinental paths.

EMERGENCY and CONTINGENCY HF NETS are pre-planned. Posts maintain published net frequencies, schedules ('net times'), call-ups, and alternate frequencies so stations can converge even when nothing else works. Discipline is enforced by a NET CONTROL STATION (NCS) that opens and closes the net, calls the roll, grants permission to transmit, directs traffic, and keeps the frequency orderly — essential when many stations share one channel under stress.

Good VOICE PROCEDURE is what makes weak, noisy HF intelligible. Use the NATO/ITU PHONETIC ALPHABET (Alfa, Bravo, Charlie … Zulu) to spell call signs and difficult words, and standard PROWORDS to carry meaning unambiguously: OVER (I'm done, your turn), OUT (transmission/contact ended — never 'over and out'), ROGER (received), WILCO (received and will comply), SAY AGAIN (repeat — never 'repeat,' which calls for artillery in some procedures), I SAY AGAIN (I'm repeating), AFFIRMATIVE/NEGATIVE, WAIT/STAND BY, and SPELL/I SPELL. Keep transmissions short, speak slightly slowly, and listen before transmitting. Finally, keep a STATION LOG: time (in UTC/Zulu), frequency, stations worked, traffic passed, and signal reports — required for accountability, troubleshooting propagation, and reconstructing events.
keyPoints: HF needs no satellites/repeaters/host telecom, making it the resilient long-haul fallback in PACE
ALE automatically sounds and scores authorized channels and picks the best one to reach a station, using Link Quality Analysis
Frequency rule of thumb: higher frequencies by day, lower by night; work just below the MUF
Higher solar flux/sunspots support higher usable frequencies and better long-haul paths; low flux/winter nights push you lower
Propagation prediction tools use solar indices and ionospheric models to forecast optimum working frequency by hour and path
HF data/email use ARQ error-correcting protocols (detect errors, request retransmission) for reliable text over fading channels
NVIS uses a low horizontal antenna on lower HF bands for regional coverage with no skip zone; DX uses higher/directional, low-angle antennas
Contingency HF nets have pre-planned frequencies, schedules, and alternates so stations can converge when nothing else works
A Net Control Station (NCS) opens/closes the net, calls roll, grants permission to transmit, and directs traffic for discipline
Use the NATO phonetic alphabet and prowords (OVER, OUT, ROGER, WILCO, SAY AGAIN); keep a UTC/Zulu station log of frequencies, stations, and traffic

flashcards.front: What is ALE (Automatic Link Establishment)?
flashcards.back: An HF feature that automatically sounds and scores authorized channels and selects the best one to link with a called station.

flashcards.front: Roughly how does ALE choose a channel?
flashcards.back: By measuring link quality on candidate frequencies (e.g., a Link Quality Analysis score) and picking the best-scoring channel.

flashcards.front: Day vs. night HF frequency rule of thumb?
flashcards.back: Use higher frequencies during the day and lower frequencies at night, staying just below the MUF.

flashcards.front: How does solar flux affect HF?
flashcards.back: Higher solar flux/sunspot activity raises usable frequencies and improves long-haul propagation; low flux pushes you to lower bands.

flashcards.front: What do HF propagation prediction tools provide?
flashcards.back: Forecasts of the optimum working frequency between two points by hour, using ionospheric models driven by solar indices.

flashcards.front: What does ARQ do in HF data modes?
flashcards.back: Automatic Repeat reQuest detects errors and requests retransmission of bad blocks, giving reliable text/email over noisy, fading HF.

flashcards.front: What is NVIS used for?
flashcards.back: Near Vertical Incidence Skywave gives reliable regional coverage (out to a few hundred km) with no skip zone, good over rough terrain.

flashcards.front: What antenna setup supports NVIS?
flashcards.back: A low horizontal antenna (about 0.1–0.25 wavelength high, e.g., a low dipole) on lower HF bands so energy radiates nearly straight up.

flashcards.front: What antenna favors HF DX (long-haul)?
flashcards.back: Higher and/or directional antennas (beams) that produce low takeoff angles for multi-hop intercontinental paths.

flashcards.front: What does a Net Control Station (NCS) do?
flashcards.back: Opens and closes the net, calls the roll, grants permission to transmit, directs traffic, and keeps the frequency orderly.

flashcards.front: Spell 'DTOT' in the NATO phonetic alphabet.
flashcards.back: Delta - Tango - Oscar - Tango.

flashcards.front: What do the prowords OVER and OUT mean?
flashcards.back: OVER = I have finished, go ahead; OUT = transmission/contact is ended. Never combine as 'over and out.'

flashcards.front: Difference between ROGER and WILCO?
flashcards.back: ROGER = received and understood; WILCO = received, understood, and will comply.

flashcards.front: Why say 'SAY AGAIN' instead of 'repeat'?
flashcards.back: 'SAY AGAIN' is the proword to ask for a retransmission; 'repeat' has other meanings in some procedures, so it is avoided.

flashcards.front: What should an HF station log record?
flashcards.back: Time in UTC/Zulu, frequency, stations worked, traffic passed, and signal reports — for accountability and troubleshooting.

flashcards.front: Why is HF a key part of the PACE plan?
flashcards.back: It works without satellites, repeaters, or host-nation infrastructure, so it remains available when primary networks fail.
